title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
short_title: Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill makes the government's watchdog agency study how well the Department of Veterans Affairs takes care of women veterans going through menopause and related health issues. The watchdog must write a report that tells the public what they found and gives ideas on how to make the care better. After getting this report, the Department of Veterans Affairs has to make a plan that follows the watchdog's suggestions to improve menopause care for women veterans. The goal is to make sure women veterans can get better and easier access to the medical help they need during menopause.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Improving Menopause Care for Veterans Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Comptroller General Study and Report on Menopause Care Furnished by Department of Veterans Affairs
sections.text: The Comptroller General of the United States (the government's main auditor) must do a study about medical care that the Department of Veterans Affairs provides to women veterans going through menopause and related health changes. This includes perimenopause (the time before menopause starts), genitourinary syndrome of menopause (bladder and intimate health problems caused by menopause), and all stages of menopause. The study will look at care provided under sections seventeen oh three and seventeen ten of title thirty eight of the United States Code (the laws that govern veterans' medical care). The Comptroller General must make a public report about this study within eighteen months after this law is passed. The report must include several things. First, it must describe what menopause care the Department of Veterans Affairs currently provides. Second, it must review the Department's rules and procedures for training doctors, diagnosing and treating menopause conditions in women veterans, and sending veterans to outside doctors when needed. Third, it must check whether veterans can get enough team-based care to treat menopause effects. Fourth, it must review how well the Department educates veterans about available menopause care, including treatment choices and possible benefits and risks. Fifth, it must evaluate how good the menopause care is by looking at veteran feedback, checking if treatments work, and seeing if veterans have ways to solve problems with their care. Sixth, it must review how the Department uses research to make menopause care better, including developing new treatments. Finally, it must give recommendations for improving menopause care. Within six months after the report is made public, the Secretary of Veterans Affairs must give Congress a plan to follow the report's recommendations, improve the quality of menopause care, and make it easier for veterans to get menopause care.
